questions.scenario: The release runbook requires a signed artifact and a successful staging checkout. The artifact exists, but provenance verification fails before production. The release window closes in 20 minutes. What should happen?

options.text: Proceed after independently verifying the registry digest and record the failed provenance check for follow-up.
Have the release owner approve a one-time bypass and attach the failed verification output to the release record.
Rebuild the same commit in the production job and deploy only if the new artifact receives valid provenance.
Stop in pre-flight and repair or replace the release evidence before production changes.

questions.explanation: Pre-flight exists to fail cheaply before traffic changes. Digest verification proves identity, not trusted origin, and a signed bypass weakens a mandatory supply-chain gate just as surely as an unrecorded one. A production rebuild creates a different artifact that has not passed staging. The safe path is to repair the evidence or replace the candidate and repeat its gates. See [SLSA provenance](https://slsa.dev/spec/v1.0/provenance).

questions.scenario: A deploy script creates a new target group every time it runs. The first run times out after creation, and the retry creates a second target group with a different name. Which redesign makes the step safely retryable?

options.text: Record the generated target group name as a workflow output and require operators to supply it when retrying.
Use a release-derived name, check current state, create only if missing, then verify.
Delete any target group created by a timed-out run before allowing the workflow to retry.
Use a new random suffix on each retry and clean up duplicate target groups after promotion.

questions.explanation: An idempotent step converges on one desired state across retries. Stable identity plus read, compare, apply, and verify survives a timeout after creation. Passing an output can help only when the failed run persisted it. Delete-before-retry risks removing a resource that is already serving traffic, while random names defer rather than solve ambiguity. See [GitHub Actions: adding scripts to workflows](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/add-scripts).

questions.scenario: Immediately after deployment, one synthetic checkout succeeds. Fifteen minutes later, p95 latency and payment declines rise under real traffic. Which runbook improvement would have caught the release before closure?

options.text: Require both the smoke transaction and a defined post-flight watch window with service and business thresholds.
Run a larger synthetic suite immediately after deployment and close the release when every transaction passes.
Keep the release open until infrastructure saturation remains stable, but treat payment declines as a separate product incident.
Extend the deployment job timeout so it remains active while engineers watch dashboards informally.

questions.explanation: A smoke suite proves selected paths at one moment. A defined watch window checks service and business behavior under real traffic and catches slower regressions. Infrastructure-only watching misses the payment outcome, while an open job without thresholds or ownership does not create a decision gate. See [Prometheus alerting practices](https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/alerting/).

questions.scenario: The canary error gate fails automatically, while payment authorization is slightly lower but still within an ambiguous range. How should the runbook divide machine and human decisions?

options.text: Automation should continue while a human recalculates the numeric threshold using the current release data.
Automation should stop only when both the technical threshold and the business signal fail in the same window.
Automation should enforce clear stop rules; an accountable owner should decide ambiguous signals with the release context.
The release owner should override the failed error gate when the business signal remains inside its confidence interval.

questions.explanation: Machines should enforce predefined stop rules consistently, while an accountable human owns context-heavy signals that the runbook marks as ambiguous. Reopening the numeric threshold after it fails turns a gate into negotiation. Requiring two failures or overriding one after the fact increases exposure beyond the declared policy. See [Google SRE incident response](https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/).

questions.scenario: Platform confirms the traffic split is correct, application logs show a new exception, and product sees checkout abandonment. The runbook lists dashboards but no decision owner. What is missing?

options.text: A single composite health score that combines platform, application, and product signals.
A severity matrix that determines the recovery action without naming an accountable decision maker.
Separate platform and product approval steps, with either team able to continue the release independently.
Named ownership for go, pause, rollback, and incident handoff decisions.

questions.explanation: Evidence and severity rules do not make a decision by themselves. The runbook must assign who can pause, promote, recover, and hand control to incident response. A composite score can hide disagreement between signals, while independent approvals can leave the release without one accountable owner. See [Google SRE incident response](https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/).

questions.scenario: A payments team has completed a production canary for checkout-api. The new version now serves 100 percent of traffic, the watch window ended without a threshold breach, and the incident responder must be able to reconstruct the release months later without consulting mutable tags or current dashboards. Which items belong in the final release record?

options.text: The workflow run URL and the mutable production image tag, with details reconstructed from the current systems when needed.
Artifact digest, source commit, and provenance result.
Staging evidence, production approval, and canary gate results.
The final approver and traffic state, but omit the previous recovery target after the watch window passes.
Runtime config identity, final traffic state, watch-window evidence, and the previous recovery target.

questions.explanation: A useful record connects the artifact to its build, gates, approval, runtime configuration, observed production behavior, and recovery target. Workflow retention and mutable tags cannot guarantee later reconstruction. The previous target remains part of the audit and recovery history even after the immediate watch window closes. See [GitHub Actions deployment environments](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/deploy-to-environment).
